name: Sea Turtles
tagline: Ancient ocean travelers that outlasted the dinosaurs — but can they survive us?
intro: Turtles have been gliding through the oceans for millions and millions of years. They come ashore only to breed and lay eggs, so most of their lives remains a mystery to us. These gentle creatures have outlived the dinosaurs, but their numbers are shrinking every year. Can they survive sharing this planet with humans?

facts.label: Species
facts.value: There are eight species of sea turtles, and every one of them seems to be dwindling.

facts.label: Diet
facts.value: The green turtle eats mostly plants; other types of turtles eat jellyfish, crustaceans, and small fish.

facts.label: Migration
facts.value: Leatherback turtles venture as far north as Greenland and Iceland in search of jellyfish, then migrate back to tropical beaches.

facts.label: Ancient
facts.value: Sea turtles have been gliding through the oceans for millions and millions of years — they outlived the dinosaurs.

facts.label: Did you know
facts.value: Baby turtles naturally head toward moonlight reflecting off the water, and can be led astray by manmade lights.

facts.label: Danger
facts.value: Sea turtles die from swallowing plastic bags, which look just like jellyfish underwater.

topics.title: Types of turtles
topics.text: There are eight species of sea turtles, and every one of them seems to be dwindling. A few species, like the gravely endangered Kemp's Ridley turtle, are rarely seen today. Sea turtles, especially the green turtle, are valued for their flesh, oil, and eggs. The hawksbill turtle is also killed for its beautiful shell, which is sold in many places as "tortoiseshell." But even turtles that are not hunted by humans are endangered by the loss of shoreline habitats such as mangrove swamps, which provide them with shelter and food, and by the loss of their wild nesting beaches to development.

captions.label: Tortoiseshell
captions.text: Hawksbill shell sold in many places as "tortoiseshell"

topics.title: Turtle food
topics.text: Different turtles like different kinds of food. The green turtle—named for the color of its body fat, and not for that of its skin—eats mostly plants. Other types of turtles eat jellyfish, crustaceans, and small fish. Sadly, many turtles die each year from swallowing manmade items they can't digest.

captions.label: Treacherous trash
captions.text: The combination of plastic bags and seawater is a deadly one for many kinds of marine animals, including turtles. Underwater, a plastic bag looks a lot like a jellyfish, a natural food item for many creatures. Swallowing plastic is usually a fatal error for any animal. Sea turtles also die from swallowing tar balls, which result from oil spills in the ocean.

captions.label: Turtle delicacy
captions.text: Most sea turtles would gladly gulp down a jellyfish.

captions.label: Plastic bags and trash on ocean beach
captions.text: Plastic bags and trash on ocean beach

topics.title: Reptiles of the sea
topics.text: About 200 million years ago reptiles ruled the land, skies, and seas. Besides turtles, only a few reptiles can now be found in the oceans. Here are three to keep an eye out for.

topics.title: The amazing leatherback
topics.text: Leatherback turtles, so named because they have soft, leathery shells, are amazing travelers. They venture as far north as Greenland and Iceland in search of the jellyfish they crave, then migrate back to tropical beaches to mate and lay their eggs. Imagine swimming thousands of miles every year!

topics.title: Nesting beaches
topics.text: Sea turtles nest on pristine tropical coasts. They need quiet beaches with gentle slopes so they can climb out of the water; warm, clean sand in which to lay their eggs; and freedom from predators that might kill them or eat their eggs. And when hatching time arrives, it's best if it's dark out: baby turtles naturally head toward moonlight reflecting off the water, and they can be led astray by manmade lights.
